title-group.article-title: Impact of COVID-19 lived experiences on future influenza pandemic worry: a cross-sectional survey in China

contrib-group.aff: https://ror.org/041tqx430grid.496809.a0000 0004 1760 1080School of Health Services and Wellness, Ningbo College of Health Sciences, 51 Xuefu Road, Ningbo, Zhejiang China

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Against the backdrop of repeated warnings regarding the inevitability of future influenza pandemics and the critical importance of public preparedness, the aim of this study was to explore public worry about a future influenza pandemic and its influencing factors in the post-coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID−19) era, particularly focusing on the role of lived experiences during the COVID−19 pandemic.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: An online cross-sectional study was conducted between October and December 2024. Overall, 1254 valid samples were finally included. Descriptive analysis and hierarchical logistic regression were performed to examine the associations between potential influencing factors and public worry about a future influenza pandemic.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Approximately 48.49% of the participants reported being worried about a future influenza pandemic. The final model of hierarchical logistic regression revealed that sex, educational level, having one or more children in the family, lived experiences (repeated infections, long-COVID, medical resource shortages), and risk perceptions (perceived likelihood and severity) were associated with worry about a future influenza pandemic.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study reveals that lived experiences during the COVID−19 pandemic are associated with increased worry about a future influenza pandemic by the public. The factors identified can be used to guide the development of evidence-based risk communication and preparedness strategies to foster public participation. Crucial baseline data were also provided from a specific period to monitor changes in public perceptions of influenza pandemic threats over time.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12889-025-25704-7.

institution-wrap.institution: Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Project

funding-source.institution: Key Project of Ningbo Public Welfare Research Plan

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The influenza virus poses a worldwide threat to human health, triggering epidemics that are unpredictable yet occur repeatedly [1–4]. Over the past century, five global viral pandemics have occurred—one caused by the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2, responsible for coronavirus disease 2019 [COVID-19]) [5], and the other four triggered by influenza A viruses [1, 6]. The pathogens of avian influenza are the subtypes of influenza A virus, and their adaptability to various animal hosts highlights the risk of transmission to humans [7]. No sustained human-to-human transmission has occurred; however, human infection with avian influenza remains a very concerning issue, as all influenza pandemics in human history to date have virus genes derived from the avian influenza virus (AIV) [2, 7]. Several potential new candidate variants of AIV have raised alerts about an influenza pandemic [8]. According to a study in TheLancet Infectious Diseases, the next influenza pandemic virus is likely to originate from spillover events involving birds—recognised as the natural reservoir of all influenza viruses—or pigs, where avianand human influenza viruses can coexist, thereby facilitating viral recombination [9]. Given that China is a high-risk region for avian influenza outbreaks and human infections [2, 7], it is necessary to raise public vigilance regarding a potential pandemic risk of human avian influenza in China.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, a series of non-pharmaceutical interventions to curb the spread of the disease was also effective in reducing the spread of the influenza virus [10–12]. From 2020 to 2021, the prevalence of seasonal influenza remained extremely low in China and globally [13]. This significant reduction in influenza activity resulted in a decline in population-level immunity to influenza viruses and a corresponding increase in susceptibility to infection [13, 14]. In December 2022, China ended its Zero-COVID Policy (During the COVID-19 pandemic, China implemented a targeted "dynamic zero-COVID strategy" between early 2020 and late 2022. This approach included large-scale nucleic acid testing, large-scale quarantines, extensive lockdowns and the use of health QR codes for entry into public spaces. The central objectives were to minimise COVID-19 infections, reduce severe cases and fatalities, safeguard public health, and protect vulnerable groups amid the global pandemic) [15]. Subsequently, the intensity of influenza activity rebounded, with the intensity in 2023 the influenza activity intensity was higher than that of the year before the COVID-19 pandemic [13]. This influenza resurgence overlapped with multiple other respiratory infectious diseases, causing a prolonged epidemic cycle that had serious impacts on public health, and revived concerns regarding a pandemic potential of influenza [1].

Indeed, both before and after the COVID-19 pandemic, scientists have repeatedly emphasised the inevitability of a future influenza pandemic and underscored the crucial importance of public preparedness [1, 3, 9].

The COVID-19 pandemic represents as the most severe threat to global health in history. During the COVID-19 pandemic, people face uncertain opportunities for infections, recurrent infection, hospitalisation, and even death. Even if there was recovery from the SARS-CoV-2 infection, many people still experienced long-term clinical sequelae [16]. Owing to the rapid increase in the number of patients with COVID-19 in a short time, difficulties such as a shortage of medical resources, a lack of epidemic prevention materials, insufficient treatment capacity, and inability to access medical consultation and hospital admission were encountered by many people [16, 17]. Beyond physical health impacts, the COVID-19 pandemic also exerted far-reaching impacts on the mental health of individuals; worry was prevalent among the public during this period [18, 19].

Worry and risk perception are relevant psychological factors that influence preventive health behaviours [20]. Worry, also known as affective risk perception[21], which is stimulated by the anticipating a negative outcome that is uncertain and may happen in the future [22]. Worry was an important predictor of more responsible behaviour during the COVID-19 pandemic [23]. People may be aware of the risks they face, but if they are not worried about them, preventive measures may not be taken [24]. Risk perception can affect worry, and thus predict subsequent intentions and behaviors [25], emphasising the role of emotions experienced during decision-making [26]. Studies indicate that factors such as age, gender, education level, income level, occupation, and health condition are associated with viral pandemic-related worry [19, 27].

The world may witness another pandemic similar to COVID-19 in the coming decades [28, 29]. Yet despite lessons learned from COVID-19, global readiness for an influenza pandemic remains insufficient [3], whether the COVID-19 pandemic has reshaped people's awareness and concerns about similar crises remains unclear. This underscores the need to thoroughly understand the factors driving public worry about the pandemic: historical records of pandemic-related losses highlight the value of identifying these factors to guide future public engagement in crisis prevention and risk communication. Notably, the World Health Organisation (WHO) has already designated public engagement and communication as a key priority for enhancing future pandemic responses [30].

A number of theories have been developed to explain the relationship between risk perception, emotions such as worry, and behaviors. Protection Motivation Theory reveals that changes in behavior are driven by fear, which is regulated by the perception of threats or risks [31]. It is believed in the Prospect Theory that a person's judgment of events is not based on absolute results, but on reference [30]. Those who had specific adverse lived experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic may fear facing similar experiences if they take normal life as a reference point, thus showing worry. While the impact of COVID-19 on people's psychology and emotional states has been extensively explored, only a few studies have focused on how individuals' unique experiences shape their worry about similar crises. To design targeted interventions that promote mental health and bolster future preparedness, identifying these influencing factors is essential. In this study, the Protection Motivation Theory and Prospect Theory were combined to explore the public worry about a future influenza pandemic and its influencing factors, particularly focusing on the role of lived experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic. The following hypotheses were tested:
H1: Individuals who experienced specific negative events during the COVID-19 pandemic will have a higher likelihood of worrying about a future influenza pandemic.
H2: Individuals with higher levels of risk perception will be more likely to worry about a future influenza pandemic.

To comprehensively explore the factors influencing public worry about a future influenza pandemic, key sociodemographic characteristics, chronic non-communicable disease (CNCD) status, and specific career and family-related factors were also included as variables in this study.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The study did not involve clinical data, human specimens, or animal experiments, nor was personal information collected; hence, ethical review was waived by the Ningbo College of Health Sciences. All participants were informed of the objectives of the study. Data were anonymously collected, and all methods were conducted, following relevant ethical guidelines and regulations.

sec.title: Study design and data Collection
sec.p: This study is a sub-investigation derived from a larger study titled “Research on the Mechanism of Vulnerability Formation and Governance Strategies of Human Avian Influenza Risk Response System - Taking Zhejiang Province as an Example”, funded by the Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Project. A cross-sectional, questionnaire-based study was conducted between October and December 2024, using two channels in China: WenJuanXing (https://www.wjx.cn/), a professional platform for electronic questionnaire design and data collection widely used in academic research, and WeChat, a mainstream social networking application.

A convenience sampling method was adopted. Specifically, including three community physicians from health service centres (one each from Ningbo, Wenzhou, and Quzhou) and three physicians from village health clinics (one each from the aforementioned three cities) in Zhejiang Province. These recruited physicians were subsequently requested to distribute the electronic link to the questionnaire to their community WeChat groups, which included their respective community residents and typically used for issuing community notices. In the introduction section of the online questionnaire (located between the title and the questionnaire body), we explained the purpose of the survey, the inclusion criteria, and instructions for completing the questionnaire. We reminded the participants that completing and submitting the questionnaire would be considered informed consent. People in the WeChat group could voluntarily decide whether or not to respond. The inclusion criteria were (1) ≥ 18 years, (2) residing in the selected community for at least 1 year, and (3) voluntary participation and the ability to complete the entire questionnaire.

The respondents’ identifiers were not collected to ensure confidentiality throughout the study. Only completed questionnaires could be uploaded to the survey platform. The questionnaire could only be filled out once using each IP address. Logically incorrect responses were considered invalid and excluded. In total, 1254 valid questionnaires were finally collected.

sec.title: Outcome variable
sec.p: The dependent variable in this study was respondents’ self-assessed worry about a future influenza pandemic. “Worry” was measured using a single adapted item from the Brief Illness Perception Questionnaire [32], which has been widely used in risk perception studies, including those focusing on the COVID−19 pandemic [33]. Participants were asked: “How worried are you about a possible future influenza pandemic?” Responses were recorded on a 5-point Likert scale (1–5), where 1 represented “very worried”, and 5 represented “not worried at all”. For the purposes of modelling, this variable was dichotomised to distinguish between worried and non-worried respondents: those who scored 1 or 2 on the Likert scale were classified as “worried”, while those with scores of 3, 4, or 5 were classified as “not worried”.

sec.title: Sociodemographic factors
sec.p: The following sociodemographic data were collected: sex, age, educational background, marital status, residential area, monthly income, and employment status.

sec.title: Career and family-related factors
sec.p: Given the potential influence of specific occupational roles or routine contact with vulnerable populations on on worry by individuals, participants were asked to respond to four binary (“yes/no”) questions: whether they (1) worked as healthcare workers; (2) were employed in elderly care or long-term care institutions; (3) held positions in childcare institutions; and (4) had children under 3 years in their household.

sec.title: Chronic non-communicable diseases
sec.p: Building on previous research, which has shown that individuals with chronic non-communicable diseases (CNCDs) were disproportionately impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic [34], we also assessed the status of CNCDs. Participants were asked to self-report their history of CNCDs, including hypertension, diabetes, coronary heart disease, malignant tumours, stroke, asthma, chronic pulmonary obstruction, kidney disease, autoimmune diseases (e.g., lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis), or other CNCDs reported by themselves. Participants who reported having at least one of the above CNCDs were classified as “individuals with CNCDs”.

sec.title: Risk perceptions
sec.p: Risk perceptions were assessed using two indicators adapted from previous research [21]: (i) perceived probability and (ii) perceived severity. Perceived probability was measured by asking the following questions: “Do you think an influenza pandemic is likely to occur in the next few decades?“. Responses were recorded on a 5-point Likert scale ranging from 1 (“very high probability”) to 5 (“very low probability”). Perceived severity was assessed with a single item: “What do you think the severity of the illness would be if humans were infected with a new type of influenza virus?“. Responses were collected on a 5-point Likert scale ranging from 1 (“Very severe”) to 5 (“Not severe at all”). For analytical purposes, both variables were dichotomised into “high” (combining categories 1 and 2) and “not high” (categories 3, 4, and 5).

sec.title: Lived experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic
sec.p: The measure of lived experiences during the COVID−19 pandemic comprises three dimensions that were highly prevalent and significantly impacted individuals’ physical and mental health: repeated infections, medical resource shortages, and long-COVID [35–37]. Repeated infection was assessed by asking participants the number of times they had confirmed SARS-CoV−2 infections throughout the pandemic. Those reporting two or more infections were classified into the repeated infection group. Medical resource shortage experiences were assessed using the following question: “Have you ever experienced the hospital capacity crisis caused by COVID−19, which has brought difficulties for you or your family to see a doctor or be hospitalised?“. Participants responded to this item using a binary “yes/no” answer. Long-COVID was measured from participants’ reported symptoms of COVID−19 infection and the duration of these symptoms. We adopt the definition endorsed by the WHO: long-COVID is a condition occurring in patients with a history of probable or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, which typically develops 3 months from the onset of symptoms, persists for at least 2 months, and cannot be explained by alternative diagnoses [38]. Long-COVID primarily includes the presence of at least one symptom, such as fatigue, dyspnoea, cognitive impairment/brain fog, postexertional malaise, memory issues, musculoskeletal pain/spasms, cough, sleep disturbances, tachycardia/palpitations, altered smell/taste perception, headache, chest pain, and depression.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Descriptive statistics were analysed using frequencies and percentages for variables. Chi-square test was performed to assess statistical significance of independent variables. Variables with p < 0.20 were included in the multivariate analysis.

Hierarchical logistic regression models were used to test the impact of COVID−19 lived experiences variables on influenza pandemic worry. Hierarchical logistic regression was conducted with four models. Model 1 incorporatesed demographic variables and established a baseline; Model 2 added occupational and family related factors, as well as chronic non communicable diseases, to control for their confounding effects; Model 3 added lived experiences during the COVID−19 pandemic to observe whether these experiences contributeed significantly to the model; Model 4 further added risk perceptions to observe whether the effect of COVID−19 experiences were still statistically significant. Odds ratios (OR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for OR were calculated. Statistical analyses were performed with SPSS version 24.0, and p < 0.05 was defined as significant.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The characteristics of the respondents are presented in Table 1. Of the respondents, approximately 48.49% expressed feeling worry about a future influenza pandemic. Most respondents were women (65.87%), married (80.38%), had a high school or above education level (76.56%), reported a monthly income of 3000–5999 yuan (28.47%), and lived in urban areas (51.67%). Most respondents believed that the possibility of an influenza pandemic occurring in the next few decades was high (62.92%) and that human infection with the novel influenza virus was severe (59.25%). Regarding experiences during COVID−19 pandemic, 38.76%, 18.50%, and 36.84% of the respondents reported having been infected with COVID−19 twice or more, experiencing long COVID, and having experienced a hospital capacity shortages, respectively.

tr.td: Having one or more children under 3 years
Staff of elderly care institutions, long-term care institutions, or welfare homes
Childcare institutions or primary school staff
Perceived consequences of the influenza pandemic

sec.p: Bivariate analysis results are presented in Table 1. The results showed that gender, age, monthly income, educational background, having one or more children under 3 years old, long-COVID, repeated infection, experience of medical resource shortages, perceived probability, and perceived severity reflected statistical differences in different groups (P < 0.05).

Variables with p < 0.20 in Table 1 were included in the hierarchical logistic regression. Table 2 shows the results of factors related to influenza pandemic worry. All four logistic regression models are significant (p < 0. 001), and the ‘−2 Log likelihood’ of each model are given (model 1: 1700.209, model 2: 1692.765, model 3:1621.395, and model 4: 1203.500). The optimal model (Model 4) results show that compared to men, women (OR = 0.669; CI = 0.493–0.909) were more likely to report worry about an influenza pandemic. Worry was also associated with educational level: among participants, those with an educational level of undergraduate or above (OR = 0.619; CI = 0.402–0.954) were more likely to report influenza pandemic worry. During the COVID−19 pandemic, participants who had a history of repeated infection (OR = 3.623; CI = 1.099–11.949), long-COVID (OR = 1.527; CI = 1.135–2.054), or experienced medical resource shortages (OR = 1.469; CI = 1.022–2.110) were more likely to report worry; thus, Hypothesis 1 was supported. Perceived likelihood (OR = 4.593; 95% CI = 3.309–6.376) and perceived severity (OR = 8.239; 95% CI = 6.000–11.314) had been identified as the two factors strongest associated with influenza pandemic worry; therefore, Hypothesis 2 was supported.

caption.p: Logistic regression analysis of factors associated with influenza pandemic worry

tr.td: Staff of elderly care institutions, long-term care institutions, or welfare homes
Having one or more children under 3 years
Perceived consequences of the influenza pandemic

sec.p: It is worth noting that COVID−19 lived experiences remained statistically significant across different model specifications, independent of other covariates included. This indicates that the association between COVID−19 lived experiences and influenza pandemic worry is not substantially confounded by other factors included in the models, confirming these COVID−19 experiences are independent influencing factors on influenza pandemic worry.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Viral outbreaks are increasing rapidly owing to climate change, anthropogenic environmental changes, and frequent contact between humans and disease-carrying animals. The possibility of extreme epidemics similar to that of COVID−19, such as the influenza pandemic, is increasing [29]. The survey was conducted more than a year after the WHO announced that the COVID−19 epidemic was no longer a “public health emergency of international concern”. In this study, COVID−19 lived experiences, risk perceptions, having one or more children under 3 years of age in the family, being a woman, and a higher education level are associated with influenza pandemic worry.

Approximately half of the participants reported worry about a future influenza pandemic in this study. A review of global research on public concern about the 2009 H1N1 influenza pandemic revealed that, with the exception of Saudi Arabia, where the proportion of individuals expressing concern reached 54.3%, the corresponding figure across other regions ranged from 2 to 36%. Additionally, the findings revealed that public concern about the 2009 H1N1 influenza pandemic declined as the pandemic progressed [39]. A comparison of data from before and after the 2009 H1N1 influenza pandemic revealed a decline in public influenza pandemic worry, from 45.5% in 2007 to 32.5% in 2009–2010 (3 months after the peak of the H1N1 2009 pandemic and 1 month after the pattern of cases had declined to normal seasonal levels) [40]. The reasonable explanation for the decrease in the level of worry is that the 2009 H1N1 influenza pandemic did not meet people’s expectations of a severe, terrifying, and widespread influenza outbreak. Some studies suggest that the 2009 H1N1 influenza was a false pandemic [41]. Therefore, the level of worry about a future influenza pandemic in this study was higher than that in previous studies, which may be related to the timing of the investigation—the survey took place less than 2 years after the end of the COVID−19 pandemic, and there was also a rebound of seasonal influenza during this period. Continuous attention should be paid to whether the worry about future similar crises will change over time in the public. This study provides a benchmark for comparing future research on worry about an influenza pandemic and risk perception.

sec.title: Lived experiences during the COVID−19 pandemic
sec.p: An important finding of this study is that certain lived experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic significantly influenced worry about a future influenza pandemic in the public and exerted an independent impact on these public concerns. In this study, 38.76% of the participants reported having contracted COVID-19 twice or more. COVID-19 reinfection is associated with an increased risk of all-cause mortality and adverse health outcomes [42]. According to a global analysis, the cumulative prevalence of long-COVID ranges from 9 to 63% and is about 6-fold higher than that of similar post-viral infection conditions [16]. In this study, long-COVID prevalence was 18.50%. Long-COVID is a debilitating post-infection multisystem condition, impairing one’s ability to perform daily activities and potentially causing lifelong consequences [43]. Therefore, those who have experienced COVID−19 reinfection or long-COVID may have a deeper concern about a future influenza pandemic in which they may face similar health damage. In addition, during the COVID−19 pandemic, there were instances where a sharp surge in infections over a short period overwhelmed and even paralysed healthcare systems [37, 44]. Personal or familial experiences of being unable to access medical resources because of such circumstances have further amplified the worry about potential similar crises in the future. These lived experiences once again emphasise the necessity of preparing for future influenza pandemics and enhancing healthcare resilience.

It was confirmed in this study that worry about a future influenza pandemic is partly driven by personal lived experiences during previous pandemics. History shows that the high level of attention garnered during the COVID−19 pandemic will soon fade; however, this study indicates that the potential impact of the pandemic may persist. In the field of natural hazards, experience is recognised as a key factor influencing risk preparedness, with direct and indirect experiences playing roles in shaping future preparation decisions and actions [45, 46]. Thus, the scope of research on the impact of lived experiences was expanded in this study.

sec.title: Risk Perceptions
sec.p: The relationship between risk perceptions and worry is usually highly correlated [33, 47, 48]. In this study, perceived probability and perceived severity were identified as the two important factors influencing influenza pandemic worry. This correlation underscores a critical opportunity to proactively address future pandemic threats—one that hinges on understanding public responses to such risks, as risk perceptions is a core component of how communities engage with preparedness efforts. To drive meaningful public participation, conveying the genuine risk and severity of a influenza pandemic is essential. For policymakers, this offers a clear roadmap: pairing transparent risk communication with demonstrations of a robust, well-prepared public health system can channel public worry into a catalyst for constructive preparedness rather than anxiety.

sec.title: Sociodemographic factors
sec.p: Women were more likely to report worry about a future influenza pandemic in this study. The sex differences in stress responses may be a reason, as women have lower tolerance for negative stress than men [49]. Reportedly, women are more likely to express concerns about COVID−19 [19]. The inconsistency in previous research is the relationship between education level and worry. It has been previously suggested that individuals with higher levels of education may have more resources to cope with COVID-19 and are less concerned about it [19]. However, in this study, the opposite result was found: participants with a higher level of education were more likely to be worried. A possible explanation is that limited understanding of the complex biological mechanisms underlying the occurrence of influenza pandemics, combined with its seasonality and confusion with the common cold, may lead individuals with lower levels of education to underestimate its severity, while those with higher education levels may be more likely to be aware of its severe implications. Another finding of this study is that participants with children under 3 years at home were more likely to be worried. Reports show that families with young children have increased concerns about the pandemic [19]. Concerns about family members were also a studied topic about the virus pandemic, especially vulnerable groups who are considered more susceptible to infections or complications. However, an Australian survey found no significant differences in risk perception of a future influenza pandemic between men and women, nor between individuals with children and those without [27]. The background and stage of the pandemic in these studies differ, so people adapt their worry to fit the changing context [40].

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study has some limitations that need to be mentioned. First, the use of a cross-sectional survey does not establish causality and only shows a snapshot of influenza pandemicrelated worry at a particular time. Second, the survey was conducted online, although considerable efforts were made to reduce bias in online surveys by obtaining a larger sample size (over 1000) and balancing the sample size between urban and rural areas; nonetheless, some sample biases (a larger proportion of higher education and female respondents) still existed. Our non-random sample is not representative of the general public in China, which limits its generalisability. Third, the concerns of the public about the influenza pandemic may be influenced by many other complex factors not identified in this study. These factors may include public trust in medicine development capabilities and the government’s ability to respond to public health crises. Fourth, self-reported COVID−19 experience data may have memory bias. These factors also limit the generalisability of the study findings, which should be interpreted with caution. In the future, large-scale longitudinal studies should be conducted to determine whether the impact of the COVID−19 pandemic experience on similar pandemic identification and judgment changes over time.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study highlights that lived experiences during the COVID−19 pandemic are associated with increased worry about future influenza pandemics among the public. Given the inherent unpredictability of the influenza virus, effective pandemic management will depend on proactive public planning and preparedness. Therefore, the factors identified in this research can be used to guide the development of evidence-based risk communication and preparedness strategies to foster public participation. Furthermore, future measures should specifically address the needs of vulnerable populations to enhance the overall effectiveness of these policies. Finally, this study also provides crucial baseline data from a specific period for monitoring changes in public perceptions of influenza pandemic threats over time.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Baohua Liu, Xiaozhuo Zhu and Yuchen Ying contributed equally to this work.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We would like to convey our sincere appreciation to the individuals who have aided us during the process of data collection and analysis.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Baohua Liu, Lingli Jiang, and Xiaozhuo Zhu planned the study. Baohua Liu, Hongguo Zhou, Yuchen Ying and Zhenzhen Xu were responsible for the data collections in Study. Baohua Liu had the main responsibility for writing the main manuscript text. Baohua Liu, Laiyou Li, Feng Wang ran the statistical analyses. All authors commented on and revised the text. The author(s) read and approved the final manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This project received funding from Zhejiang Province Public Welfare Technology Application Research Project (LGF22G030018) and Key Project of Ningbo Public Welfare Research Plan (2023S027).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets used and analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was conducted according to the guidelines laid down in the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the Ethics Committee of Ningbo College of Health Sciences. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

element-citation.article-title: Preparing amidst a pandemic
